text: The speed of a transverse wave on a string with tension \(T\) and linear mass density \(\mu\) is:
explanation: Wave speed on string: \( v = \sqrt{T/\mu} \). Higher tension or lower mass density → faster wave.
name: Wave Speed on String

text: The relationship between wave speed \(v\), frequency \(f\), and wavelength \(\lambda\) is:
explanation: Fundamental wave relation: \( v = f\lambda \). Or \( v = \omega/k \) where \(k = 2\pi/\lambda\) is wave number.
name: Wave Speed Relation

text: The speed of sound in a gas (Newton-Laplace formula) is:
explanation: Laplace correction: \( v = \sqrt{\gamma P/\rho} \). Newton's formula lacked \(\gamma\) (assumed isothermal; actually adiabatic). \( v_{sound} \approx 332\,\text{m/s} \) at 0°C.
name: Speed of Sound in Gas

text: The Doppler effect formula for source moving towards stationary observer is:
explanation: Source approaching observer: \( f' = f\frac{v}{v - v_s} > f \) (higher pitch). Source receding: \( f' = f\frac{v}{v + v_s} < f \).
name: Doppler Effect - Source Moving

text: In a stationary wave, nodes are points where:
explanation: Nodes: points of zero amplitude in standing waves. Antinodes: maximum amplitude. Distance between adjacent nodes = \(\lambda/2\).
name: Nodes in Standing Waves

text: The frequency of the \(n\)th harmonic in a closed pipe (one end closed) is:
explanation: Closed pipe: fundamental \( f_1 = v/4L \). Only odd harmonics: \( f_n = (2n-1)v/4L \). Open pipe: all harmonics present.
name: Closed Pipe Harmonics

text: Beats are produced when two sound waves of frequencies \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) are superposed. Beat frequency equals:
explanation: Beat frequency \( f_{beat} = |f_1 - f_2| \). Perceptible as beats when difference is small (< ~7 Hz for human ear).
name: Beat Frequency

text: The fundamental frequency of an open pipe of length \(L\) is:
explanation: Open pipe: antinodes at both ends. \( \lambda_1 = 2L \Rightarrow f_1 = v/\lambda_1 = v/2L \). All harmonics present.
name: Open Pipe Fundamental Frequency

text: The wave equation \( \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = v^2 \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} \) is satisfied by:
options: Any wave function of form \( y = f(x \pm vt) \)
explanation: The general solution is \( y = f(x-vt) + g(x+vt) \) — any shape travelling in \(+x\) or \(-x\) direction.
name: Wave Equation

text: The intensity of a wave is proportional to:
explanation: Wave intensity \( I \propto A^2 f^2 \). Doubling amplitude quadruples intensity. Intensity in W/m².
name: Wave Intensity and Amplitude

text: The speed of sound increases with temperature as:
explanation: \( v = \sqrt{\gamma RT/M} \propto \sqrt{T} \). For every 1°C rise, speed of sound in air increases by ≈0.6 m/s.
name: Speed of Sound vs Temperature

options: A medium with elasticity (bulk modulus): liquids, gases, solids
explanation: Longitudinal waves (compression-rarefaction): require medium with bulk modulus. Can travel in solid, liquid, gas. Sound is longitudinal.
name: Medium for Longitudinal Waves

text: The condition for constructive interference between two waves is path difference:
explanation: Constructive interference: path difference \( = 0, \lambda, 2\lambda, ... = n\lambda \). Destructive: \( = \lambda/2, 3\lambda/2, ... = (2n+1)\lambda/2 \).
name: Constructive Interference Condition

text: End correction in organ pipes accounts for:
options: The effective length being slightly greater than physical length (antinode outside open end)
explanation: End correction \(e \approx 0.6r\) (r = radius). Effective length \( L_{eff} = L + e \) or \( L + 2e \) (both ends open). Antinodes form slightly outside the open end.
name: End Correction in Pipes

text: Melde's experiment demonstrates the formation of standing waves on a string. The number of loops \(p\) is related to tension \(T\) by:
explanation: Melde's experiment: \( p = \frac{L}{\lambda/2} \), frequency \( f = p\frac{v}{2L} = p\frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{T/\mu} \). So \( p \propto 1/\sqrt{T} \) at fixed \(f\).
name: Melde's Experiment
